name: List.Nonempty

unions.name: ListNonempty
unions.comment: A list type that must contain at least one element

values.name: all
values.comment:
Same as `List.all`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> all (\n -> n < 2) == False

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> all (\n -> n < 5) == True

values.name: any
values.comment:
Same as `List.any`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> any (\n -> n > 2) == True

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> any (\n -> n > 5) == False

values.name: append
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: appendElem
values.comment:
Append a value to become the new last element.

    append 4 [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 1, 2, 3, 4 ]

values.name: concat
values.comment:
Similar to `List.concat`

    fromPair (fromPair 0 [ 1 ]) [ fromPair 2 [ 3 ] ]
        |> concat
        |> toList
        == [ 0, 1, 2, 3 ]

    fromPair (fromPair 0 []) []
        |> concat
        |> toList
        == [ 0 ]

values.name: concatMap
values.comment:
Similar to `List.concatMap`

    concatMap (\n -> fromPair n [ n ]) (fromPair 0 [ 1, 2 ])
        |> toList
        == [ 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2 ]
values.type: (a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: drop
values.comment:
Return all elements except for the first N elements. Always includes the last element, regardless of
the value of N. If this behavior is undesired, use `toList` and `List.drop`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> drop 2 |> toList == [ 2, 3 ]

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> drop 10 |> toList == [ 3 ]

values.name: dropLast
values.comment:
Return all elements except for the last N elements. Always includes the first element, regardless of
the value of N. If this behavior is undesired, use `toList >> List.reverse >> List.drop n >> List.reverse`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> dropLast 2 |> toList == [ 2, 3 ]

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> dropLast 10 |> toList == [ 3 ]

values.name: filter
values.comment:
Similar to `List.filter`. Returns `List` because we cannot guarantee
any elements will pass the test.

    fromPair 1 [ 2, 3, 4 ] |> filter (\n -> n > 2) == [ 3, 4 ]

    fromPair 1 [ 2, 3, 4 ] |> filter (\n -> n > 20) == []

values.name: filterMap
values.comment:
Similar to `List.filterMap`. Also returns `List`.

    fromPair "3.5" [ "cat", "4", "ball" ]
        |> filterMap String.toFloat
        == [ 3.5, 4 ]
values.type: (a -> Maybe.Maybe b) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.List b

values.name: foldl
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> b

values.name: foldr
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> b

values.name: fromList
values.comment:
Try to create a non-empty list from an ordinary list.
Returns Nothing if input list is empty.

    fromList [ 0, 1, 2 ] == Just (fromPair 0 [ 1, 2 ])

    fromList [] == Nothing

values.name: fromPair
values.comment:
Create a non-empty list from an element and a list.

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> toList == [ 0, 1, 2, 3 ]

values.name: fromTuple
values.comment:
Create a non-empty list from a tuple of element and list

    fromTuple ( 0, [ 1, 2, 3 ] ) |> toList == [ 0, 1, 2, 3 ]

values.name: head
values.comment:
Return the first element. Unlike `List.head`, doesn't need to return a Maybe.

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> head == 0

values.name: indexedMap
values.comment:
Equivalent to `List.indexedMap`

    fromPair "a" [ "b", "c", "d" ]
        |> indexedMap Tuple.pair
        |> toList
        == [ ( 0, "a" ), ( 1, "b" ), ( 2, "c" ), ( 3, "d" ) ]
values.type: (Basics.Int -> a -> b) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: isSingleton
values.comment:
Whether a non-empty list has only a single element

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> isSingleton == False

    fromPair 0 [] |> isSingleton == True

values.name: last
values.comment:
Return the last element. Doesn't need to return a Maybe.

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> head == 3

values.name: map
values.comment:
Equivalent to `List.map`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> map String.fromInt
        |> toList
        == [ "0", "1", "2", "3" ]

values.name: map2
values.comment:
Similar to `List.map2`

    map2 (\a b -> a ++ "-" ++ b)
        (fromPair "a" [ "b", "c" ])
        (fromPair "1" [ "2", "3" ])
        |> toList
        == [ "a-1", "b-2", "c-3" ]
values.type: (a -> b -> result) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty result

values.name: map3
values.comment:
Similar to `List.map3`

    map3 (\a b c -> ( a, b, c ))
        (fromPair 0 [ 1 ])
        (fromPair 2 [ 3 ])
        (fromPair 4 [ 5 ])
        |> toList
        == [ ( 0, 2, 4 ), ( 1, 3, 5 ) ]
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> result) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty result

values.name: map4
values.comment:
Similar to `List.map4`

    map3 (\a b c -> ( a, b, c ))
        (fromPair 0 [ 1 ])
        (fromPair 2 [ 3 ])
        (fromPair 4 [ 5 ])
        |> toList
        == [ ( 0, 2, 4 ), ( 1, 3, 5 ) ]
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> result) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty d -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty result

values.name: map5
values.comment:
Similar to `List.map5`

    map3 (\a b c -> ( a, b, c ))
        (fromPair 0 [ 1 ])
        (fromPair 2 [ 3 ])
        (fromPair 4 [ 5 ])
        |> toList
        == [ ( 0, 2, 4 ), ( 1, 3, 5 ) ]
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> result) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty d -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty e -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty result

values.name: maximum
values.comment:
Similar to `List.maximum`, but doesn't need to return a Maybe type

     fromPair 0 [1, 2, 3] |> maximum == 3

     fromPair 2 [] |> maximum == 2

values.name: member
values.comment:
Same as `List.member`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> member 2 == True

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> member 5 == False

values.name: minimum
values.comment:
Similar to `List.minimum`, but doesn't need to return a Maybe type

     fromPair 0 [1, 2, 3] |> minimum == 0

     fromPair 2 [] |> minimum == 2

values.name: popFirst
values.comment:
Pop the first element.

    popFirst 4 [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 1, 2, 3, 4 ]

-> Maybe (a, ListNonempty a)
-> (a, Maybe (ListNonempty a))
-> (a, Either (List a) (ListNonempty a))

values.name: popLast
values.comment:
Pop the last element.

    popLast 4 [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 1, 2, 3, 4 ]

-> Maybe (a, ListNonempty a)
-> (a, Maybe (ListNonempty a))
-> (a, Either (List a) (ListNonempty a))

values.name: prependElem
values.comment:
Prepend a value to become the new first element.

    prepend 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 0, 1, 2, 3 ]

values.name: range
values.comment:
Works slightly differently from `List.range`. First provided number
will be the first number in the non-empty list, second provided number
will be the last number, and it can ascend or descend.

Equivalent to `List.range a b` when `a<=b`, equal to `singleton a`
if `a>b`

    range 3 6 |> toList == [ 3, 4, 5, 6 ]

    range 3 3 |> toList == [ 3 ]

    range 6 3 |> toList == [ 6, 5, 4, 3 ]

values.name: repeat
values.comment:
Similar to `List.repeat`, except this is guaranteed to contain
at least one element, regardless of the integer provided. Otherwise
a Maybe return value would be required. If this behavior is desired,
use `List.repeat` plus `fromList`

    repeat 3 "a" |> toList == [ "a", "a", "a" ]

    repeat 0 "a" |> toList == [ "a" ]

values.name: rest
values.comment:
Return everything except the last element. Doesn't need to return a Maybe.

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> rest == [ 0, 1, 2 ]

values.name: singleton
values.comment:
Create a non-empty list containing just a single element

    singleton 0

values.name: sortBy
values.comment:
Similar to `List.sortBy`

    fromPair { x = 1, y = 1 } [ { x = 0, y = 2 }, { x = 2, y = 0 } ]
        |> sortBy .x
        |> toList
        == [ { x = 0, y = 2 }, { x = 1, y = 1 }, { x = 2, y = 0 } ]

    fromPair { x = 1, y = 1 } [ { x = 0, y = 2 }, { x = 2, y = 0 } ]
        |> sortBy .y
        |> toList
        == [ { x = 2, y = 0 }, { x = 1, y = 1 }, { x = 0, y = 2 } ]

values.name: sortWith
values.comment:
Similar to `List.sortWith`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3, 4 ]
        |> sortWith (\a b -> compare b a)
        |> toList
        == [ 4, 3, 2, 1, 0 ]
values.type: (a -> a -> Basics.Order) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: tail
values.comment:
Return everything except the first element. Unlike `List.tail`, doesn't need to return a Maybe.

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> tail == [ 1, 2, 3 ]

values.name: take
values.comment:
Return the first N elements. Always includes the first element, regardless of
the value of N. If this behavior is undesired, use `toList` and `List.take`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> take 2 |> toList == [ 0, 1 ]

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> take 0 |> toList == [ 0 ]

values.name: takeLast
values.comment:
Return the last N elements. Always includes the last element, regardless of
the value of N. If this behavior is undesired, use `toList >> List.reverse >> List.take n >> List.reverse`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> takeLast 2 |> toList == [ 2, 3 ]

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> takeLast 0 |> toList == [ 3 ]

values.name: toList
values.comment:
Convert to a list

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> toList == [ 0, 1, 2, 3 ]

values.name: toTuple
values.comment:
Deconstruct a non-empty list into a tuple of element and list

    fromTuple ( 0, [ 1, 2, 3 ] )
        |> toTuple
        == ( 0, [ 1, 2, 3 ] )

values.name: uncons
values.comment:
Equivalent to `(\elems -> (head elems, tail elems))`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> uncons == ( 0, [ 1, 2, 3 ] )

values.name: unconsLast
values.comment:
Equivalent to `(\elems -> (rest elems, last elems))`

    fromPair 0 [ 1, 2, 3 ] |> unconsLast == ( [ 0, 1, 2 ], 3 )

values.name: unzip2
values.comment:
Similar to `List.unzip`.

    fromPair ( "a", 1 ) [ ( "b", 2 ), ( "c", 3 ) ]
        |> unzip2
        == ( fromPair "a" [ "b", "c" ]
           , fromPair 1 [ 2, 3 ]
           )
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty ( a, b ) -> ( List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b )

values.name: unzip3
values.comment:
Similar to `unzip2`, but with 3-tuples.

    fromPair ( 1, 2, 3 ) [ ( 4, 5, 6 ), ( 7, 8, 9 ) ]
        |> unzip3
        == ( fromPair 1 [ 4, 7 ]
           , fromPair 2 [ 5, 8 ]
           , fromPair 3 [ 6, 9 ]
           )
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty ( a, b, c ) -> ( List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c )

name: List.Nonempty.Extra

values.name: appendList
values.comment:
Similar to append, except taking an ordinary list as second argument.

    appendList (fromPair 0 [ 1, 2 ]) [ 3, 4, 5 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 ]
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.List a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: gatherWith
values.type: (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: gatherWithList
values.type: (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> List.List a -> List.List (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: greedyGroupsOfWithStep
values.type: Basics.Int -> Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: groupWhile
values.type: (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: groupWhileList
values.type: (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> List.List a -> List.List (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: groupsOfVarying
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: groupsOfWithStep
values.type: Basics.Int -> Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty (List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a)

values.name: indexedFoldl
values.type: (Basics.Int -> a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> b

values.name: indexedFoldr
values.type: (Basics.Int -> a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> b

values.name: lift2
values.type: (a -> b -> c) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c

values.name: lift3
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty d

values.name: lift4
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty d -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty e

values.name: mapAccuml
values.type: (a -> b -> ( a, c )) -> a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> ( a, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c )

values.name: mapAccumr
values.type: (a -> b -> ( a, c )) -> a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b -> ( a, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty c )

values.name: partition
values.comment:
Equivalent to `List.partition`. We can't have any (simple) guarantees about
the lengths of the two returned lists.

    fromPair 2 [ 5, 1, 4 ]
        |> partition (\n -> n > 2)
        == ( [ 5, 4 ], [ 2, 1 ] )
values.type: (a -> Basics.Bool) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> ( List.List a, List.List a )

values.name: prependList
values.comment:
Similar to append, except taking an ordinary list as first argument.

    appendList (fromPair 0 [ 1, 2 ]) [ 3, 4, 5 ]
        |> toList
        == [ 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 ]
values.type: List.List a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: scanl
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: scanl1
values.comment: Similar to `List.Extra.scanl1` except doesn't need to return a `Maybe`. The function is `(a -> a -> a)` though, instead of `(a -> b -> b)`.
values.type: (a -> a -> a) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: scanlList
values.comment: Similar to `scanl`, except takes a `List` as input. Output is still guaranteed to be non-empty.
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.List a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: scanr
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: scanr1
values.comment: Similar to `List.Extra.scanr1` except doesn't need to return a `Maybe`. The function is `(a -> a -> a)` though, instead of `(a -> b -> b)`.
values.type: (a -> a -> a) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: scanrList
values.comment: Similar to `scanr`, except takes a `List` as input. Output is still guaranteed to be non-empty.
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> List.List a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty b

values.name: select
values.comment: Similar to `List.Extra.select`. Output type was chosen because the output of this function is likely intended to be used as separate pieces. If output type of `ListNonempty (ListNonempty a)` is desired, use `select >> map fromTuple`.
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty ( a, List.List a )

values.name: selectSplit
values.type: List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty ( List.List a, a, List.List a )

values.name: setAt
values.type: Basics.Int -> a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: setIf
values.type: (a -> Basics.Bool) -> a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: setIfIndex
values.type: (Basics.Int -> Basics.Bool) -> a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: splitAtRest
values.type: Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> ( List.List a, List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a )

values.name: splitAtTail
values.type: Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> ( List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a, List.List a )

values.name: swapAt
values.type: Basics.Int -> Basics.Int -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: updateAt
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> a) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: updateIf
values.type: (a -> Basics.Bool) -> (a -> a) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a

values.name: updateIfIndex
values.type: (Basics.Int -> Basics.Bool) -> (a -> a) -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a -> List.Nonempty.ListNonempty a
